analogy: Think of "Solar Radiation Heat Balance and Temperature" through the chapter's own opening images: ATMOSPHERIC CIRCULATION AND WEATHER SYSTEMS E arlier Chapter 8 described the uneven distribution of temperature over the surface of the earth. Air expands when heated and gets compressed when cooled. This results in variations in the atmospheric pressure. The result is that it causes the... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Solar Radiation Heat Balance and Temperature is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that aTMOSPHERIC CIRCULATION AND WEATHER SYSTEMS E arlier Chapter 8 described the uneven distribution of temperature over the surface of the earth.

The text explains that air expands when heated and gets compressed when cooled.

The text explains that this results in variations in the atmospheric pressure.

The text explains that the result is that it causes the movement of air from high pressure to low pressure, setting the air in motion.

The text explains that you already know that air in horizontal motion is wind.

The text explains that atmospheric pressure also determines when the air will rise or sink.

The text explains that the wind redistributes the heat and moisture across the planet, thereby, maintaining a constant temperature for the planet as a whole.

The text explains that the vertical rising of moist air cools it down to form the clouds and bring precipitation.

The text explains that this chapter has been devoted to explain the causes of pressure differences, the forces that control the atmospheric circulation, the turbulent pattern of wind, the formation of air masses, the disturbed weather when air masses interact with each other and the phenomenon of violent tropical storms.

The text explains that aTMOSPHERIC PRESSURE Do you realise that our body is subjected to a lot of air pressure.

The text explains that as one moves up the air gets varified and one feels breathless.

The text explains that the weight of a column of air contained in a unit area from the mean sea level to the top of the atmosphere is called the atmospheric pressure.

The text explains that the atmospheric pressure is expressed in units of milibar.

The text explains that at sea level the average atmospheric pressure is 1,013.2 milibar.

The text explains that due to gravity the air at the surface is denser and hence has higher pressure.

The text explains that air pressure is measured with the help of a mercury barometer or the aneroid barometer.

The text explains that at any elevation it varies from place to place and its variation is the primary cause of air motion, i.e. wind which moves from high pressure areas to low pressure areas.

The text explains that vertical Variation of Pressure In the lower atmosphere the pressure decreases rapidly with height.

The text explains that 3 10 km 265.00 – 49.7 The vertical pressure gradient force is much larger than that of the horizontal pressure gradient.

The text explains that aTMOSPHERIC CIRCULATION AND WEATHER SYSTEMS 77 Horizontal Distribution of Pressure Small differences in pressure are highly significant in terms of the wind direction and purposes of comparison.

The text explains that the sea level pressure distribution is shown on weather maps.

The text explains that figure 9.1 shows the patterns of isobars corresponding to pressure systems.

The text explains that low- pressure system is enclosed by one or more isobars with the lowest pressure in the centre.

The text explains that high-pressure system is also enclosed by one or more isobars with the highest pressure in the centre.

The text explains that world Distribution of Sea Level Pressure The world distribution of sea level pressure in January and July has been shown in Figures 9.2 and 9.3.

The text explains that near the equator the sea level pressure is low and the area is known as equatorial low.

The text explains that along 30° N and 30o S are found the high-pressure areas known as the subtropical highs.

The text explains that further pole wards along 60o N and 60o S, the low-pressure belts are termed as the sub polar lows.

The text explains that near the poles the pressure is high and it is known as the polar high.

The text explains that these pressure belts are not permanent Figure 9.2 : Distribution of pressure (in millibars) — January Figure 9.1 : Isobars, pressure and wind systems in Northern Hemisphere velocity.

The text explains that horizontal distribution of pressure is studied by drawing isobars at constant levels.

The text explains that isobars are lines connecting places having equal pressure.

The text explains that in order to eliminate the effect of altitude on pressure, it is measured at any station after being reduced to sea level for

The text explains that fUNDAMENTALS OF PHYSICAL GEOGRAPHY 78 Pressure Gradient Force The differences in atmospheric pressure produces a force.

The text explains that the rate of change of pressure with respect to distance is the pressure gradient.

The text explains that the pressure gradient is strong where the isobars are close to each other and is weak where the isobars are apart.

The text explains that coriolis Force The rotation of the earth about its axis affects the direction of the wind.

The text explains that it deflects the wind to the right direction in the northern hemisphere and in nature.

The text explains that forces Affecting the Velocity and Direction of Wind You already know that the air is set in motion due to the differences in atmospheric pressure.

The text explains that the wind blows from high pressure to low pressure.

The text explains that aTMOSPHERIC CIRCULATION AND WEATHER SYSTEMS 85 3.

The text explains that (i) Discuss the factors affecting the speed and direction of wind.

The text explains that (ii) Draw a simplified diagram to show the general circulation of the atmosphere over the globe.

The text explains that what are the possible reasons for the formation of subtropical high pressure over 30o N and S latitudes?

The text explains that (iii) Why does tropical cyclone originate over the seas?

The text explains that in which part of the tropical cyclone do torrential rains and high velocity winds blow and why?

The text explains that (ii) Read the section on weather in any newspaper, preferably, one having a map showing a satellite picture.

The text explains that attempt to infer the atmospheric circulation from the distribution of clouds.

The text explains that compare the forecast given in the newspaper with the TV coverage, if you have access to TV.

The text explains that estimate, how many days in a week was the forecast were accurate.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Fundamentals of Physical Geography, Chapter 9: Solar Radiation Heat Balance and Temperature

prelims_traps: Do not treat ATMOSPHERIC CIRCULATION and AND WEATHER SYSTEMS as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Solar Radiation Heat Balance and Temperature; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Solar Radiation Heat Balance and Temperature through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use ATMOSPHERIC CIRCULATION and AND WEATHER SYSTEMS to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
